methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $315M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Lilac Solutions is a Series C-stage climate and energy technology company that has raised $315M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer universe of three same-sector, same-stage private companies (KoBold Metals, Electric Hydrogen, and Amogy) alongside one relevant public comparable.

Two triangulation methods apply here. The stage-progression method uses total capital raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.7x multiplier typical for Series C advancement, yielding $536M. The peer-median method benchmarks against the three identified comparables, which span $700M to $2.0B in valuation with a median of $1.0B. This reflects the wide dispersion in valuations within the climate tech sector at this stage, where company-specific technology maturity and capital intensity drive material variance.

Synthesizing these two approaches produces a valuation range of $455M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median point of $768M. The 2.5x spread between endpoints reflects genuine uncertainty: the peer median pulls significantly higher than the stage-progression method, suggesting either that Lilac's capital efficiency or technology position may warrant a premium relative to historical step-up patterns, or conversely, that peer valuations reflect sector-wide exuberance. Key sensitivities include the company's path to near-term revenue, capital requirements for scale, and shifts in climate tech investor appetite. The peer set, while internally consistent, remains small; widening the comparable universe would reduce range uncertainty.
